Today Seifried Estate is the region's largest winery, with a welcoming cellar door and popular restaurant. The Sweet Agnes Riesling (named after Agnes Seifried) is a benchmark dessert wine, and the whole range — Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Noir, Gewürztraminer — offers excellent value.

reviews.summary: The pioneer story adds depth. The restaurant is reliable and the wines offer great value. Some find it less intimate than smaller Nelson wineries.

pois.name: Golden Bear Brewing Co
pois.short: Mapua Wharf craft brewery — small-batch beers with estuary views on the Tasman Bay waterfront.
pois.commentary: Golden Bear is one of the gems of the Mapua Wharf precinct, brewing small-batch beers using Nelson hops and local water. The tap selection changes regularly — expect hop-forward pales and IPAs, sessionable lagers, and occasional wild ferments. The wharf-side seating with Tasman Bay views is idyllic, and it pairs perfectly with smoked fish from the neighbouring Smokehouse.

reviews.summary: Great location with good small-batch beers. The Mapua Wharf setting adds charm. Limited seating in peak summer.

pois.name: The Smokehouse
pois.short: Mapua Wharf's famous smokehouse — hot-smoked fish, mussels and seafood platters on the waterfront.
pois.commentary: The Smokehouse at Mapua Wharf has been smoking fish for decades, and the technique shows. The hot-smoked salmon, smoked blue cod and mussel platters are all excellent, served at waterfront tables overlooking the Waimea estuary. It's simple food done brilliantly — the smoke flavour is delicate, not overpowering, and the fish is always moist. A Nelson Tasman must-visit, especially after cycling the Great Taste Trail.

reviews.summary: The smoked fish quality is outstanding. The waterfront setting is lovely. Can be very busy in peak summer — arrive early for the best selection.

pois.name: Jester House Café
pois.short: Quirky garden café near Tasman — eel feeding, native gardens and excellent brunch in a rural setting.
pois.commentary: Jester House is one of Nelson Tasman's most characterful cafés — set in lush native gardens where you can feed tame eels in the stream, play lawn games, and wander through themed garden areas. The food is well above standard café fare, with seasonal produce, excellent cabinet food and good coffee. It's especially popular with families but charming for everyone. The rural Tasman location is a pleasant drive from Nelson.

reviews.summary: The eel feeding and gardens delight children and adults. The food quality surprises. A fun, quirky stop on the way to Abel Tasman.

pois.name: Abel Tasman Tonga Island — Fur Seal Colony
pois.short: NZ fur seal breeding colony on Tonga Island — kayak past basking seals in Abel Tasman National Park.
pois.commentary: Tonga Island, just off the Abel Tasman coast, hosts a significant NZ fur seal breeding colony. Kayakers paddle past the haul-out and can observe seals from the water at a safe distance. During breeding season (Nov-Jan), pups play in the shallows.

reviews.summary: Highlights of any Abel Tasman kayak trip. The seals are incredibly photogenic. Maintain 20m distance.

pois.name: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary
pois.short: NZ's largest predator-fenced urban sanctuary — kaka, bellbird, and South Island robin minutes from Nelson CBD.
pois.commentary: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary is enclosed by a 14 km predator-proof fence, making it NZ's largest fenced urban sanctuary. Kaka, South Island robin, bellbird, and other species flourish in the regenerating bush. Walking tracks range from gentle strolls to hill climbs. Just 10 minutes from central Nelson.

reviews.summary: Outstanding urban sanctuary. The kaka are delightful. Easy access from Nelson. Tracks for all fitness levels.

pois.name: Pepin Island — Little Blue Penguin Colony
pois.short: Little blue penguins nest around the rocky shores of Pepin Island near Cable Bay.
pois.commentary: Pepin Island at the end of Cable Bay Road hosts a colony of little blue penguins that nest in rock crevices around the headland. Dusk visits during breeding season offer the best chance of seeing penguins coming ashore. The walk around the headland is scenic.

reviews.summary: Pleasant coastal walk with penguin potential. Timing at dusk is key. Cable Bay area is beautiful.

pois.name: Kahurangi National Park — Long-Tailed Bat Habitat
pois.short: Ancient forests harboring NZ's endangered long-tailed bats — listen for echolocation clicks at dusk.
pois.commentary: Kahurangi National Park's old-growth beech and podocarp forests provide important habitat for the long-tailed bat (pekapeka). The Oparara and Wangapeka areas are known bat habitats. Dusk at forest edges offers the best chance of seeing bats foraging. DOC bat monitors can help detection.

reviews.summary: Wild and remote. Bat sightings require patience and the right conditions. The forest is spectacular regardless.

pois.name: Wainui Falls — Abel Tasman Freshwater Ecology
pois.short: Bush walk to a beautiful waterfall with native fish, eels, and kōura (freshwater crayfish) in the pools.
pois.commentary: The Wainui Falls track near Abel Tasman passes through lush podocarp forest to a photogenic waterfall. The stream and pools below contain longfin eels, kōkopu, and kōura (freshwater crayfish). Bush birds including bellbird and tui are abundant along the track.

reviews.summary: Beautiful walk to a pretty waterfall. The freshwater ecology adds interest. Easy track suitable for families.

pois.name: Lake Rotoiti Nature Recovery Project
pois.short: DOC's flagship South Island mainland island — intensive pest control brings bush birds back to honeydew beech forest.
pois.commentary: The Lake Rotoiti Nature Recovery Project in Nelson Lakes National Park covers 825 hectares of honeydew beech forest. Intensive pest control has allowed kaka, South Island robin, rifleman, and great spotted kiwi to thrive. The Bellbird Walk and Lakeside Track offer excellent birding.

reviews.summary: Exceptional birding in stunning setting. The honeydew beech forest is unique. Kaka are often seen around the lake.

pois.name: Waimea Estuary — Shorebird Haven
pois.short: Nelson's major estuary supports godwit, spoonbill, and variable oystercatcher year-round.
pois.commentary: The Waimea Estuary near Nelson is the region's most important shorebird site. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, variable oystercatcher, and pied stilt are regular visitors. The estuary margins are accessible by foot and cycle trail. Best birding at low tide.

reviews.summary: Good birding close to town. Low tide is essential. The cycle trail makes for easy exploration.

pois.name: Wharariki Beach — Seal Colony
pois.short: Dramatic wind-sculpted beach with a NZ fur seal colony in sea caves and rock arches.
pois.commentary: Wharariki Beach at the top of the South Island is one of NZ's most photogenic beaches — dramatic rock arches, sea caves, and wind-sculpted dunes. A NZ fur seal colony inhabits the offshore rock platforms and caves. The 20-minute walk crosses farmland with views to Farewell Spit.

reviews.summary: Breathtaking scenery with seals as a bonus. The rock arches are incredible. Can be very windy.

pois.name: Heaphy Track — Nikau Palm Coast Birds
pois.short: The Heaphy Track's coastal section passes through nikau palm forests alive with tui, bellbird, and weka.
pois.commentary: The Heaphy Track's coastal section between Scotts Beach and the Kohaihai River is one of NZ's most unique birding environments. Nikau palm forests extend to the shore, filled with tui, bellbird, weka, and fernbird. The blue duck (whio) inhabits the upper river sections.

reviews.summary: Iconic Great Walk with incredible birdlife on the coastal section. Multi-day commitment required.

pois.name: Oparara Basin — Cave & Forest Ecology
pois.short: Ancient limestone arches, glowworm caves, and rare cave spiders in pristine old-growth forest.
pois.commentary: The Oparara Basin near Karamea contains massive limestone arches, cave systems with glowworms, and the rare Nelson cave spider. The surrounding old-growth podocarp forest is one of the least disturbed in the country. Great spotted kiwi inhabit the forest floor.

reviews.summary: Otherworldly place. The arches are enormous. Allow time for the short walks to multiple cave entrances.

pois.name: Mapua Estuary Bird Walk
pois.short: Easy estuary walk near Mapua village — shags, herons, and oystercatchers on the tidal flats.
pois.commentary: The Mapua Estuary walkway provides easy access to productive tidal flats where pied shag, white-faced heron, variable oystercatcher, and kingfisher are regularly seen. The walk connects to the Mapua waterfront village for refreshments afterward.

reviews.summary: Pleasant walk with reliable bird sightings. Family-friendly. Combines well with Mapua village visit.

pois.name: Tōtaranui — Coastal Wildlife
pois.short: Abel Tasman golden beach with little blue penguins, weka, and rich rock pool marine life.
pois.commentary: Tōtaranui in Abel Tasman National Park offers a combination of golden sand beach, weka encounters, and little blue penguin nesting habitat along the headlands. The rock pools at each end of the beach are rich with marine invertebrates. Shearwaters and gannets visible offshore.

reviews.summary: Beautiful beach with plenty of wildlife. The weka are bold and entertaining. Great rock pools for kids.

pois.name: The Suter Art Gallery Te Aratoi o Whakatū
pois.short: Nelson's premier art gallery in beautiful Queens Gardens, with an outstanding collection of regional and national art.
pois.commentary: The Suter Art Gallery Te Aratoi o Whakatū is the Nelson region's principal public gallery, beautifully situated alongside Queens Gardens in the heart of the city. Established in 1899, it is one of New Zealand's oldest public art galleries. The gallery underwent a major $9.4 million redevelopment completed in 2016, creating modern exhibition spaces while respecting the heritage of the original building. The collection includes significant works by artists connected to the Nelson region, including Toss Woollaston, Jane Evans, and the expatriate artist Frances Hodgkins. The gallery presents a lively programme of temporary exhibitions alongside permanent collection displays. Admission is free. The Nelson region has one of the highest concentrations of practising artists per capita in New Zealand, and the gallery reflects this creative energy. The adjacent Queens Gardens are a beautiful heritage park. The on-site theatre screens films and hosts events.

reviews.summary: A really lovely gallery in a beautiful garden setting. The redeveloped spaces are excellent and the collection reflects Nelson's strong artistic heritage. Free entry and a good café make it a must-visit.

pois.name: Theatre Royal Nelson
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest surviving theatre building (1878), still a vibrant venue for performing arts in Nelson.
pois.commentary: Theatre Royal Nelson holds the distinction of being New Zealand's oldest surviving purpose-built theatre, opened in 1878 and in continuous use ever since. The intimate 246-seat auditorium retains much of its Victorian charm with ornate plasterwork, a traditional proscenium arch, and excellent sightlines from every seat. The theatre hosts a vibrant programme of professional touring shows, local amateur productions, music concerts, comedy, and community events throughout the year. It is the home venue for Nelson's active amateur theatre community. The building has been sensitively maintained and upgraded over the decades, preserving its heritage character while meeting modern safety and accessibility standards. The theatre is listed as a Category I historic place by Heritage New Zealand. Located on Rutherford Street in central Nelson, it is surrounded by restaurants and bars, making it easy to combine dinner and a show. Check the website for current performances.

reviews.summary: Such a charming historic theatre — the intimacy of the space makes every show feel special. Hard to believe this beautiful building is nearly 150 years old. A real Nelson treasure.

pois.name: Founders Heritage Park
pois.short: Living heritage park with historic buildings, vintage machinery, a brewery, and organic café in a park setting.
pois.commentary: Founders Heritage Park is a unique heritage attraction on the outskirts of Nelson, set in extensive park-like grounds containing relocated and replica historic buildings that recreate aspects of the region's pioneer past. The park includes a working printing press, a vintage fire engine collection, a historic windmill, a heritage railway, an organic garden, and various workshops demonstrating traditional crafts. The on-site Founders Brewery is a popular craft beer destination, and the organic café serves excellent food using produce from the park's gardens. The collection of heritage buildings includes cottages, commercial buildings, and industrial structures that have been rescued from demolition around the region. The park hosts regular markets, community events, and festivals. It is a great family destination with plenty to explore across the spacious grounds. Allow a couple of hours to wander through the various buildings and exhibits. Admission fees apply.

reviews.summary: A lovely heritage park with lots to explore, especially good for families. The brewery and café are excellent additions. The vintage machinery collection is impressive. A relaxing way to spend a couple of hours.

pois.name: World of WearableArt & Classic Cars Museum
pois.short: Dazzling museum showcasing winning garments from the famous WOW awards alongside a stunning classic car collection.
pois.commentary: The World of WearableArt & Classic Cars Museum (WOW) is one of Nelson's most popular attractions, housing an extraordinary collection of award-winning garments from the internationally acclaimed World of WearableArt Awards Show. The wearable art is displayed in themed galleries with theatrical lighting and dramatic staging, showcasing the incredible creativity and craftsmanship of entries spanning decades. Garments range from the spectacular to the bizarre — towering sculptural pieces, bioluminescent creations, and intricately detailed wearable sculptures. Alongside, the classic car collection features over 100 beautifully restored vehicles spanning a century of motoring history. The combination of fashion-as-art and automotive heritage creates a uniquely entertaining museum experience. The WOW competition began in Nelson in 1987 before moving to Wellington, but the museum keeps the original spirit alive. Allow at least two hours. The museum is located at Nelson Airport, convenient for arrival or departure days. Excellent gift shop.

reviews.summary: An absolute must-visit in Nelson — the garments are stunning and the presentation is world-class. The classic car collection is a bonus treat. You'll spend longer than expected here, it's that good.

pois.name: Kaiteriteri Beach
pois.short: Iconic golden sand beach and gateway to Abel Tasman National Park, famous for crystal-clear water.
pois.commentary: Kaiteriteri Beach is one of New Zealand's most beautiful and iconic beaches, famous for its distinctive golden-orange granite sand and crystal-clear turquoise water. Located on the Tasman Bay coast, it is the main gateway for water taxis and kayak trips into Abel Tasman National Park. The sheltered, north-facing beach offers warm, safe swimming conditions and is surrounded by native bush-clad headlands. The beach is divided into several coves, each with its own character — the main beach is the busiest, while Split Apple Rock and other nearby coves offer more seclusion. Kayak, paddleboard, and small boat hire are available from the beach. The Kaiteriteri Mountain Bike Park in the hills behind offers excellent riding. During summer, arrive early for parking as the beach gets very popular. The campground behind the beach is one of the most sought-after in New Zealand — book months ahead for peak season. Even on busy days, the beauty of the beach is undeniable.

reviews.summary: Absolutely gorgeous beach — the golden sand and turquoise water look tropical. Perfect launching point for Abel Tasman adventures. Get there early in summer as it gets very busy, but it's worth it.

pois.name: The Free House Nelson
pois.short: Craft beer bar housed in a converted 1886 church in central Nelson, pouring independent NZ beers.
pois.commentary: The Free House is one of New Zealand's most distinctive craft beer venues, occupying a beautifully converted 1886 Methodist church on Collingwood Street in central Nelson. The interior retains original church features including arched windows and timber beams, creating an atmospheric setting for sampling an ever-rotating selection of independent New Zealand craft beers. With 19 taps dedicated exclusively to small independent breweries, it is a showcase for the best of NZ craft beer. The venue does not brew its own beer but curates the tap lineup to feature the best current releases nationwide. A wood-fired pizza oven provides the food, and the sunny courtyard is a summer favourite. The Nelson region's concentration of hop growers and craft breweries makes this an ideal starting point for exploring the local beer scene. No bookings, just turn up.

reviews.summary: A craft beer paradise in one of New Zealand's most atmospheric venues. The rotating tap list always features interesting finds. The converted church setting adds enormous character.

pois.name: Eddyline Brewery
pois.short: Popular Nelson craft brewery with a family-friendly taproom, wood-fired pizzas, and sunny outdoor area.
pois.commentary: Eddyline Brewery has grown from a small garage operation into one of Nelson's most popular craft breweries. Their taproom on Nile Street offers the full range of Eddyline beers on tap, from the crisp Pilsner and hoppy IPA to seasonal specialties. The brewery uses Nelson-grown hops, capitalising on the region's status as New Zealand's hop-growing heartland. The menu centres on excellent wood-fired pizzas designed to pair with the beers, and the spacious outdoor area with picnic tables is family-friendly with space for children to play. Live music features on weekends during summer. The brewery name and branding reflects a passion for kayaking, and the adventurous spirit carries through to their brewing. Tastings paddles let you sample across the range before committing. A lively, welcoming venue that captures the Nelson lifestyle.

reviews.summary: Great craft beer and excellent wood-fired pizzas in a relaxed setting. The outdoor area is perfect on a sunny Nelson day. Family-friendly atmosphere with genuine craft beer credentials.

pois.name: Abel Tasman Sea Kayaking
pois.short: Guided sea kayaking along the golden beaches and granite coastline of Abel Tasman National Park.
pois.commentary: Sea kayaking is one of the finest ways to experience the Abel Tasman coastline, paddling between golden sand beaches, through the turquoise waters of sheltered bays, and past sculpted granite headlands. Multiple operators offer guided trips ranging from half-day paddles to multi-day expeditions with beachside camping. The calm, sheltered waters of Tasman Bay make conditions ideal for kayaking, and the coastline is studded with accessible beaches for rest stops and swimming. Wildlife encounters are frequent, with little blue penguins, fur seals, and dolphins regularly spotted. Most trips depart from Marahau at the southern end of the park, with water taxi connections available for longer itineraries. No prior kayaking experience is necessary for guided trips, and tandem kayaks are available. Book well ahead during January and February.

reviews.summary: An unforgettable way to experience the Abel Tasman coastline. Guides are skilled and informative about marine ecology. The combination of paddling, swimming, and beach stops makes for a perfect day.

pois.name: Mapua Wharf Precinct
pois.short: Waterfront dining and tasting precinct at Mapua Wharf with seafood, craft beer, ice cream, and estuary views.
pois.commentary: Mapua Wharf is a delightful waterfront precinct at the edge of the Waimea Estuary, about 30 minutes from Nelson city. The former apple packing shed and surrounding buildings have been transformed into a collection of eateries, galleries, and food artisans that draw locals and visitors alike. The Smokehouse is the star attraction, serving freshly smoked fish and seafood from its waterside counter. Golden Bear Brewing offers craft beers brewed on site, and Jester House Cafe across the estuary is known for its tame eels. The precinct also houses galleries, a gelato maker, and a small aquarium. The Mapua Ferry connects the wharf to Rabbit Island, a popular swimming and cycling beach. The Great Taste Trail cycle route passes through Mapua, making the wharf a natural refreshment stop. On a sunny day, eating smoked fish and sipping local beer on the wharf edge with views across the estuary is hard to beat.

reviews.summary: A wonderful waterfront precinct with fantastic smoked seafood and craft beer. The estuary setting is beautiful and the atmosphere is relaxed. A highlight of the Nelson-Tasman food scene.

pois.name: Nelson Saturday Market
pois.short: Vibrant Saturday morning market in central Nelson with fresh produce, artisan goods, and street food.
pois.commentary: The Nelson Saturday Market is one of New Zealand's best weekly markets, held every Saturday morning in the Montgomery Square car park in central Nelson. The market brings together around 60 stallholders selling fresh seasonal produce, artisan breads, cheeses, preserves, honey, organic vegetables, fresh flowers, and handmade crafts. The food court section features diverse street food options, excellent coffee, and fresh juice. The market has been running since the 1990s and reflects Nelson's reputation as a creative, food-conscious community. Local musicians often provide entertainment, and the sunny Nelson climate means outdoor browsing is a pleasure for much of the year. The market runs from approximately 8am to 1pm and is popular with both locals and visitors. Located in the heart of the city, it is easily combined with gallery and cafe visits in the surrounding streets.

reviews.summary: A vibrant and authentic weekend market with excellent produce and artisan goods. The food stalls are diverse and delicious. A perfect Saturday morning activity capturing the Nelson lifestyle.

pois.name: Split Apple Rock
pois.short: Iconic split granite boulder in Tasman Bay near the Abel Tasman coast, accessible by kayak or water taxi.
pois.commentary: Split Apple Rock (Tokangawha) is one of New Zealand's most photographed natural features, a large granite boulder split perfectly in two as if cleaved by a giant axe. The rock sits in shallow water in the Tasman Bay between Kaiteriteri and Marahau, near the entrance to Abel Tasman National Park. In Maori legend, two gods fought over the rock and each took half, explaining the clean split. Geologically, water seeped into a crack and froze, gradually wedging the boulder apart over thousands of years. The rock is best viewed up close by kayak, water taxi, or boat, though it can be glimpsed from the coastal track above. Kayak tours from Kaiteriteri often include a paddle past the rock, allowing close-up photography. The surrounding waters are clear and excellent for swimming in summer. The rock is also visible from scenic flights over the Abel Tasman coastline.

reviews.summary: A remarkable natural curiosity that photographs beautifully. Best experienced by kayak for the close-up perspective. A natural highlight of the Abel Tasman coastal area.

pois.name: Ngarua Caves
pois.short: Limestone cave with stalactites, stalagmites, and moa bones on the Takaka Hill road to Golden Bay.
pois.commentary: Ngarua Caves are a limestone cave system located at the summit of Takaka Hill on the road between Nelson and Golden Bay. The guided tour leads through decorated chambers featuring stalactites, stalagmites, flowstone formations, and the bones of the extinct moa bird, preserved where they fell into the cave thousands of years ago. The caves were formed over millions of years as rainwater dissolved the marble and limestone of the Takaka Hill geological formation. Tours last approximately 45 minutes and are conducted by knowledgeable guides who illuminate the geological processes and natural history. The cave temperature is a constant 10 degrees Celsius year-round, so bring a warm layer. Located at the Takaka Hill lookout, the caves combine naturally with the scenic drive over the hill, which offers spectacular views of both Tasman Bay and Golden Bay. A worthwhile stop that adds geological interest to the journey to Abel Tasman and Golden Bay.

reviews.summary: An interesting geological attraction that breaks up the drive over Takaka Hill nicely. The moa bones are fascinating and the formations are attractive. Guides are knowledgeable and engaging.

pois.name: The Suter Art Gallery, Nelson
pois.short: Nelson's premier art gallery since 1899 — a significant collection of New Zealand art in a recently renovated Queens Gardens setting.
pois.commentary: The Suter Art Gallery Te Aratoi o Whakatu has been Nelson's cultural anchor since 1899, founded with a bequest from Bishop Andrew Suter. The gallery underwent a major renovation and reopened in 2016 with modern exhibition spaces that complement the original building. The permanent collection includes significant works by Toss Woollaston, Jane Evans, and other artists connected to the Nelson region's strong arts tradition. Rotating exhibitions bring national and international contemporary art to the sunny city. The gallery sits in Queens Gardens, Nelson's most beautiful park, making a visit easy to combine with a stroll through the heritage rose gardens and mature trees.

reviews.summary: Art lovers appreciate the quality collection and the modern renovation. The Queens Gardens setting is lovely. Free entry makes it easy to drop in for even a short visit.

pois.name: Founders Heritage Park, Nelson
pois.short: A collection of relocated heritage buildings, a working brewery, and a miniature railway — Nelson's pioneer history brought to life.
pois.commentary: Founders Heritage Park gathers Nelson's pioneer history into one accessible site on Atawhai Drive. Heritage buildings rescued from around the region have been relocated here, including a windmill, printshop, fire station, and several colonial-era cottages. The Founders Brewery operates on site, producing craft beer in a building that fits the heritage aesthetic. A miniature railway loops through the grounds, delighting children and railway enthusiasts alike. The park hosts regular markets and events that bring the heritage buildings to life with activity. While it lacks the drama of Nelson's natural attractions, Founders offers a genuine connection to the region's colonial settlement story and works well as a family outing or a rainy-day option.

reviews.summary: Families enjoy the miniature railway and the hands-on heritage experience. The brewery is a bonus for adults. Some buildings could use more interpretive signage but the overall atmosphere is pleasant.

pois.name: Nelson Classic Car Museum
pois.short: Over 150 restored classic cars spanning a century of motoring — one of the largest collections in New Zealand.
pois.commentary: The Nelson Classic Car Museum houses over 150 immaculately restored vehicles ranging from early 1900s brass-era automobiles to muscle cars of the 1970s. The collection is privately owned and the passion of the collectors is evident in the condition of every vehicle. Highlights include rare pre-war European models, American classics, and a section dedicated to New Zealand motoring history. The museum is well-lit and the cars are displayed at ground level where you can appreciate the craftsmanship up close. Even non-car enthusiasts find themselves drawn in by the design evolution on display. It is one of those unexpected Nelson attractions that visitors stumble upon and end up spending far longer in than planned.

reviews.summary: Car enthusiasts call this a world-class collection in an unexpected location. The restoration quality is exceptional. Even non-petrolheads enjoy the design and history on display.

pois.name: Broadgreen Historic House
pois.short: A beautifully preserved 1855 cob house — one of the finest examples of early colonial domestic architecture in New Zealand.
pois.commentary: Broadgreen House in Stoke is a substantial two-storey cob (earth and straw) house built around 1855, now preserved as a museum of early Nelson colonial life. The thick cob walls have survived over 160 years of earthquakes and weather, a testament to the building technique brought from Devon by the original settlers. Inside, rooms are furnished in period style with many original and locally-sourced pieces that evoke the domestic life of a prosperous colonial family. The surrounding gardens are planted in heritage varieties and maintained by dedicated volunteers. Broadgreen offers an intimate window into how early European settlers lived in Nelson — their aspirations, their craftsmanship, and their adaptation to a new land.

reviews.summary: History lovers appreciate the well-preserved cob construction and period furnishings. The gardens are a pleasant bonus. A quiet, unhurried heritage experience away from the main tourist trail.

pois.name: Hoglund Art Glass, Richmond
pois.short: World-renowned glass blowing studio founded by Swedish artists — watch molten glass transformed into art in the Nelson sunshine.
pois.commentary: Hoglund Art Glass was established by Swedish glass artists Ola and Marie Hoglund, who brought Scandinavian glass-blowing traditions to the Nelson region's thriving arts community. The studio and gallery in Richmond allows visitors to watch the mesmerising process of glass blowing — molten glass gathered from a furnace at over 1,000 degrees Celsius, shaped by breath and hand tools into bowls, vases, and sculptural pieces. The gallery displays finished works ranging from functional tableware to large-scale art pieces exhibited in galleries internationally. The Hoglunds' work draws on both Swedish design traditions and the natural forms of New Zealand — sea, bush, and sky rendered in luminous glass. The studio is a working space, so glass-blowing demonstrations depend on production schedules.

reviews.summary: Visitors are mesmerised by the glass-blowing demonstrations. The gallery pieces are stunning and uniquely combine Scandinavian and New Zealand aesthetics. Call ahead to confirm demonstration times.

pois.name: Centre of New Zealand, Nelson
pois.short: A short walk to the geographic centre of New Zealand — marked by an obelisk on a hill with panoramic views over Nelson and Tasman Bay.
pois.commentary: The Centre of New Zealand walk climbs Botanical Hill (originally known as Tirohanga) to an obelisk marking what is traditionally called the geographic centre of the country. The designation dates to the 1870 trigonometric survey that used this hill as the central reference point for mapping New Zealand. Whether it is the true geographic centre is debatable — it depends on how you calculate it — but the walk is a Nelson essential regardless. The 30-minute climb rewards with panoramic views over the city, Tasman Bay, and the ranges beyond. The track starts from the Botanical Reserve and is well-formed if moderately steep. It is best at sunset when the light across Tasman Bay is at its most golden.

reviews.summary: The views from the top justify the short climb regardless of the geographic debate. Sunset visits are particularly recommended. A Nelson must-do that takes less than an hour.

pois.name: Cable Bay Walkway
pois.short: A coastal ridgeline walk north of Nelson — sweeping views of Tasman Bay, the Marlborough Sounds, and the Kaikoura ranges.
pois.commentary: The Cable Bay Walkway traverses the ridgeline between Glenduan and Cable Bay north of Nelson, offering some of the finest coastal views in the region. The track follows open farmland along the tops, with panoramic views across Tasman Bay to the east and Delaware Bay to the west. On clear days the vista extends to the Kaikoura ranges and the Marlborough Sounds. The walk takes about four hours one way, so most walkers arrange a car shuttle or walk to a high point and return the same way. The ridge is exposed to wind, which keeps the walk cool in summer but requires warm layers in cooler months. The walkway is named for the telegraph cable that once ran from Cable Bay to the Australian network.

reviews.summary: The ridgeline views are outstanding — one of the best coastal walks near Nelson. The open farmland terrain makes for easy walking with unobstructed panoramas. Arrange a shuttle for the full one-way experience.

pois.name: Ngarua Caves, Takaka Hill
pois.short: Limestone caves on Takaka Hill with moa bones still embedded in the floor — a guided underground journey through millions of years of geology.
pois.commentary: Ngarua Caves sit near the summit of Takaka Hill on the road to Golden Bay, offering guided tours through a limestone cave system decorated with stalactites, stalagmites, and flowstone formations. The caves' most remarkable feature is the presence of moa bones — skeletal remains of the extinct giant bird still visible in the cave floor where they fell centuries ago. Guides explain the geology of the marble mountain and the story of the moa with genuine enthusiasm. The caves maintain a constant cool temperature, making them a refreshing stop on a hot day or a dry retreat in rain. Tours run regularly and last about 45 minutes, with the cave system well-lit and accessible for most visitors. Ngarua is a natural complement to the dramatic Takaka Hill drive.

reviews.summary: The moa bones are a genuine wow moment. Guides are knowledgeable and engaging. A natural stop on the Takaka Hill drive — good for families and geology enthusiasts alike.

pois.name: Wainui Falls, Abel Tasman Foreshore
pois.short: A 22-metre waterfall cascading through nikau palm forest — one of the prettiest short walks in the Golden Bay area.
pois.commentary: Wainui Falls drops 22 metres into a rock pool surrounded by lush nikau palm forest in the hills behind Wainui Bay near Abel Tasman National Park. The walk to the falls takes about 45 minutes each way through beautiful lowland bush — nikau palms, tree ferns, and rimu create a subtropical canopy overhead. A swing bridge near the falls gives an elevated view of the cascade. The track is well-formed but has some steps and can be muddy after rain. The falls flow year-round but are most impressive after wet weather, which is conveniently common in this part of Golden Bay. Combined with a visit to Wainui Bay's wild beach, the walk makes a rewarding half-day outing from Takaka.

reviews.summary: A beautiful short walk through stunning nikau forest to an impressive waterfall. The swing bridge viewing point is a highlight. Can be muddy — good footwear recommended.

pois.name: Nelson Saturday Market
pois.short: New Zealand's best outdoor market since 1981 — local produce, artisan food, crafts, and buskers every Saturday in the Nelson sun.
pois.commentary: The Nelson Saturday Market has been running every Saturday morning since 1981, filling Montgomery Square with local produce, artisan food, crafts, and live music. The market reflects Nelson's character — organic vegetables from the Moutere hills, handmade cheeses, freshly baked sourdough, pottery, jewellery, and woodwork by local artisans. Food stalls serve everything from Thai curry to German bratwurst to fresh-squeezed juice. Buskers provide the soundtrack, and the atmosphere is relaxed, sunny, and quintessentially Nelson. The market runs from 8am to 1pm year-round, rain or shine (though Nelson's sunshine hours mean rain is the exception). It is the best way to spend a Saturday morning in the region and a genuine community gathering.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently rate this as one of New Zealand's best markets. The local produce and artisan crafts are excellent. Arrive early for the best selection and a relaxed browse.

pois.name: Heaphy Track
pois.short: New Zealand's longest Great Walk — 78 km through Kahurangi National Park from golden tussock tops to wild nikau palm coastline.
pois.commentary: The Heaphy Track is the longest of New Zealand's ten Great Walks at 78.4 km, crossing Kahurangi National Park from the Aorere Valley near Collingwood to Karamea on the West Coast. The track traverses an extraordinary range of landscapes — open tussock-covered downs at the Gouland Downs, podocarp forest in the Heaphy Valley, and a dramatic final section along a wild coast fringed with nikau palms. The Heaphy is typically walked in four to six days, with DOC huts spaced along the route. Since 2012 the track has also been open to mountain bikers between May and November. The variety of terrain and vegetation — from subalpine to subtropical — makes this arguably the most ecologically diverse of all the Great Walks.

reviews.summary: Trampers praise the Heaphy for its extraordinary variety of landscapes over four to six days. The coastal nikau palm section is a particular highlight. The mountain biking option outside summer adds versatility.

pois.name: Kaiteriteri Beach
pois.short: Golden sand and sheltered turquoise water — Kaiteriteri is the gateway beach to Abel Tasman National Park and a destination in its own right.
pois.commentary: Kaiteriteri Beach gets its distinctive golden colour from the granite-derived sand unique to this section of the Nelson coast. The beach is sheltered, safe for swimming, and backed by pohutukawa trees that blaze red in December. As the main departure point for Abel Tasman water taxis and kayak tours, Kaiteriteri buzzes with activity in summer. But the beach itself rewards a longer stay — the sand is warm, the water clear, and the setting between granite headlands is genuinely beautiful. A short walk over the headland leads to smaller, quieter Split Apple Rock beach. The Kaiteriteri Mountain Bike Park in the hills behind adds another dimension. For many visitors, Kaiteriteri is the first taste of the Abel Tasman magic.

reviews.summary: The golden sand and clear water consistently delight visitors. The beach is safe for families and beautiful for photos. Summer can get crowded — early morning or late afternoon visits are recommended.

pois.name: Te Waikoropupu Springs (Pupu Springs)
pois.short: The clearest freshwater springs on Earth — sacred to Maori and mesmerising to all who visit.
pois.commentary: Te Waikoropupu Springs near Takaka produce some of the clearest freshwater ever measured — underwater visibility exceeds 63 metres, rivalling the purest laboratory water. The springs discharge up to 14,000 litres per second from an underground system fed by rainwater filtering through the marble karst of Takaka Hill. The water is a haunting blue-green, and the 'dancing sands' where it erupts from the spring floor are hypnotic. The springs are wahi tapu — sacred — to the local iwi Manawhenua ki Mohua, and since 2020 visitors are no longer permitted to enter the water. A viewing platform allows respectful observation. The short walk from the car park passes through bush and old gold mining water races. It is one of the most extraordinary natural phenomena in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the water clarity as almost unbelievable — the springs must be seen to be appreciated. The sacred status is clearly communicated and respected. A short, easy walk suitable for everyone.

pois.name: World of WearableArt & Classic Cars Museum
pois.short: Two museums in one — spectacular WearableArt garments displayed as art alongside a world-class classic car collection.
pois.commentary: The World of WearableArt & Classic Cars Museum (WOW) in Nelson combines two seemingly unrelated collections under one roof to brilliant effect. The WearableArt section displays winning garments from the annual World of WearableArt Awards Show — extraordinary creations that push the boundaries of fashion, sculpture, and performance art. The garments are displayed in theatrical lighting with dramatic staging that brings them to life. Downstairs, the classic car collection showcases over 100 immaculate vehicles in themed displays. The juxtaposition of wearable art and automotive design actually works — both celebrate human creativity, craftsmanship, and the desire to make beautiful objects. The museum is a Nelson original and unlike anything else in the country. The annual WOW show itself has moved to Wellington, but the museum keeps the magic alive year-round.

reviews.summary: Even non-fashion visitors are blown away by the WearableArt displays. The theatrical presentation brings the garments to life. The classic car collection adds unexpected depth and appeals to a different audience.

pois.name: World of WearableArt & Classic Cars Museum
pois.short: A museum celebrating the famous WOW awards — wearable art that pushes the boundary between sculpture and fashion.
pois.commentary: The World of WearableArt (WOW) Awards started in Nelson in 1987 as a small gallery event and grew into an internationally acclaimed spectacle now staged annually in Wellington. This museum in Nelson displays winning garments from past awards — extraordinary creations made from materials ranging from cable ties to car parts to chocolate wrappers. The adjacent Classic Cars Museum houses over 140 vehicles. The displays are theatrical, with dramatic lighting and soundscapes.

reviews.summary: Visitors are amazed by the creativity and craftsmanship of the garments. The classic car collection is a bonus. Some wish there were more garments on display but quality over quantity.

pois.name: Nelson Lakes National Park
pois.short: Two glacial lakes set against forested mountains — the northern gateway to the Southern Alps.
pois.commentary: Nelson Lakes National Park contains Lakes Rotoiti and Rotoroa — two deep glacial lakes surrounded by red, silver and mountain beech forest. Lake Rotoiti is the more accessible, with short walks along the lakeshore, a DOC visitor centre at St Arnaud, and the excellent Rotoiti Nature Recovery Project. Longer tramps include the Travers-Sabine Circuit (4–7 days). The park is the northern end of the Southern Alps.

reviews.summary: The lakes are stunningly beautiful and much less crowded than other national parks. Bird life around Rotoiti is noticeably richer thanks to pest control.

pois.name: Höglund Art Glass
pois.short: Watch Swedish-born master glassblowers create stunning art glass in their Richmond studio — one of NZ’s finest craft studios.
pois.commentary: Ola and Marie Höglund moved from Sweden to Nelson in 1982, drawn by the region’s artistic community and lifestyle. Their studio produces world-class art glass and the gallery shows both functional and exhibition works. Visitors can watch the glassblowing process through the studio windows. Free to visit.

reviews.summary: Watching the glassblowing is mesmerising. The gallery pieces are beautiful. A must-visit for anyone interested in craft.

pois.name: Kaiteriteri Beach
pois.short: Golden sand, clear water and the gateway to Abel Tasman — one of the South Island’s best swimming beaches.
pois.commentary: Kaiteriteri is a golden-sand beach in Tasman Bay that serves as the main departure point for Abel Tasman water taxis, kayak tours and boat trips. But it’s also a destination in its own right — the sand is a distinctive golden-orange, the water is clear and sheltered. Split Apple Rock is nearby.

reviews.summary: Beautiful beach with distinctively golden sand. Great for families and as a base for Abel Tasman trips.

pois.name: Nelson Provincial Museum / Pupuri Taonga o Te Tai Ao
pois.short: Nelson's regional museum at Hardy Street — with significant Māori taonga, colonial-era photography collections, and the story of Te Tau Ihu's eight iwi.
pois.commentary: The Nelson Provincial Museum, Pupuri Taonga o Te Tai Ao, holds one of New Zealand's most significant regional photography collections — over 100,000 historic images from the 1860s onward. Its Māori collection includes taonga from the eight iwi of Te Tau Ihu o Te Waka a Māui (the prow of Māui's canoe), the Māori name for the Nelson-Tasman-Marlborough region. Exhibitions interpret Nelson's layered history: pre-European Māori settlement, the New Zealand Company's controversial 1841 purchase, the land confiscations, and the subsequent settlement period. The museum actively partners with local iwi in the interpretation and care of their taonga, reflecting a commitment to shared history. The building at Hardy Street is modern and accessible.

reviews.summary: The Nelson Provincial Museum is praised for the quality of its historical photography and the depth of the Māori collections. Visitors appreciate that the exhibits treat Te Tau Ihu history honestly, including the difficult aspects of colonisation. Recommended as the best introduction to the region's full story.

pois.name: Founders Heritage Park Nelson
pois.short: A heritage village park in Nelson with over 20 historic buildings, a working steam train on NZ's last surviving narrow-gauge heritage railway, and craft breweries on site.
pois.commentary: Founders Heritage Park at 87 Atawhai Drive preserves a cluster of relocated and reconstructed heritage buildings in a park setting beside Nelson Haven. The site includes a general store, farm buildings, a working forge, a 1920s printer's workshop, and a printing press still operated on demonstration days. The Nelson Railway Society operates a narrow-gauge steam and diesel railway that is one of the last operational heritage rail lines in New Zealand. The park has evolved into something of an artisan precinct — the Founders Brewing Company, one of New Zealand's original craft breweries established in 1854 (the building, not the beer), operates on site. An accessible, varied experience that bridges industrial heritage and contemporary Nelson culture.

reviews.summary: Founders Heritage Park is recommended as a relaxed half-day option in Nelson, particularly for families and railway enthusiasts. The combination of heritage buildings, a working train, and a craft brewery on site makes it unusually versatile. Reviewers suggest weekends when more buildings and demonstrations are active.

pois.name: Onetahua Marae, Pōhara
pois.short: The home marae of three iwi in Golden Bay — Ngāti Rarua, Ngāti Tama, and Te Ātiawa — with the wharenui Te Ao Marama opened in 2001.
pois.commentary: Onetahua Marae at 72 Pōhara Valley Road was established in 1986 as the focal point for three of the eight iwi of Te Tau Ihu: Ngāti Rarua, Ngāti Tama, and Te Ātiawa o Te Waka-a-Māui. The name Onetahua — 'heaped up sand' — is the Māori name for Farewell Spit, the extraordinary sand formation that defines the northern edge of Golden Bay. The wharenui Te Ao Marama ('a new light, a new dawn') was opened in January 2001, its name signalling a forward-looking, inclusive approach — the marae explicitly welcomes the wider community as well as the three home iwi. The marae anchors to the waka Tokomaru, the maunga Parapara, and the awa Waikoropupū (the sacred springs nearby). Around 300 Māori occupation sites have been recorded in Golden Bay, making this landscape among the most archaeologically rich in the South Island.

reviews.summary: Onetahua Marae is described by visitors who have been welcomed there as one of the most genuine cultural experiences in Golden Bay — the setting, the stories, and the warmth of the welcome reflect a marae that is actively alive and connected to its community. Best visited as part of a guided cultural experience.

pois.name: Te Puna Wai o Riuwaka / Riuwaka Resurgence
pois.short: A sacred pool where the Riuwaka River emerges fully formed from the base of Tākaka Hill — wāhi tapu to Te Ātiawa and Ngāti Rārua, and one of New Zealand's most unusual natural sites.
pois.commentary: Te Puna o Riuwaka is the resurgence point of the Riuwaka River, which travels underground through the Tākaka marble mountain for several kilometres before emerging in a crystal pool at the base of a cliff. The pool is recognised as wāhi tapu by Te Ātiawa and Ngāti Rārua — a sacred, supernatural place where tūpuna came to cleanse, heal, and sustain their spirits. The 400 m walk from the carpark follows the young river through native bush to the resurgence cave, where the water pours from the living rock in extraordinary volume and clarity. The site sits within Kahurangi National Park, 16 km north of Motueka. Swimming is inappropriate given the cultural status of the site. The combination of geological wonder and spiritual significance makes this one of the most layered short walks in the region.
sources_to_check: DOC Te Puna Wai o Riuwaka

reviews.summary: The Riuwaka Resurgence is described as quietly astonishing — the sight of a full river appearing from a cave in a single, clear pool leaves most visitors speechless. Those who approach it with awareness of its cultural status describe it as one of the most significant places they visit in New Zealand.

pois.name: Whakatū Marae, Atawhai
pois.short: The marae of the six land-holding iwi of Nelson-Tasman — Ngāti Kuia, Ngāti Kōata, Ngāti Rārua, Ngāti Tama, Ngāti Toa, and Te Ātiawa — opened in 1995 beside Nelson Haven.
pois.commentary: Whakatū Marae in Atawhai was opened in 1995 to serve as the collective marae for all six land-holding iwi of the Nelson region, an unusual arrangement that reflects the complex and layered tribal history of Te Tau Ihu. The wharenui Kākāti was opened on 1 April 1995. Whakatū — the Māori name for Nelson — means 'to construct' or 'to establish', and the city's name connects it to the founding of a new order following the Musket Wars migrations of the early 19th century. The marae occupies a historically significant site near the shores of Nelson Haven, close to where Māori trade and the early European settlement intersected. This is a living marae, not a visitor attraction, and any visit should be through arranged cultural tourism programmes.

reviews.summary: Whakatū Marae is described by those who have visited through cultural programmes as a place where the complexity of Te Tau Ihu history becomes personal and comprehensible. The six-iwi arrangement is explained thoughtfully, and the marae setting beside the haven is beautiful.

pois.name: Abel Tasman Sea Kayaking and Seal Colony
pois.short: Guided sea kayaking through Abel Tasman National Park's golden-sand coastline to resident fur seal colonies on Adele and Pinnacle Islands.
pois.commentary: Abel Tasman National Park's coastline is one of New Zealand's most scenic kayaking environments, combining emerald-green sea, golden granite sand beaches, and the turquoise tidal inlets of the Totaranui, Awaroa, and Bark Bay estuaries. New Zealand fur seals have established year-round colonies on the rocky islands of Adele, Pinnacle, and Tonga Island, and guided kayak tours weave between these outcrops to observe seals hauled out on the rocks and swimming around the kayaks with playful curiosity. Kaiteriteri Beach 61 kilometres from Nelson is the main departure point, and guided day trips are operated by multiple licensed operators. The warm, sheltered waters of Tasman Bay make conditions manageable for beginner paddlers in calm weather.

reviews.summary: Sea kayaking Abel Tasman is rated as one of New Zealand's great outdoor experiences, combining exceptional coastal scenery, accessible wildlife encounters, and the meditative pleasure of paddling in calm sea conditions. The fur seal interactions are a particular highlight.

pois.name: McCashin's Brewery / Stoke
pois.short: New Zealand's original craft brewery in a heritage building with tastings and tours.
pois.commentary: McCashin's Brewery in Stoke holds a special place in New Zealand beer history as the country's first craft brewery, established by Terry McCashin in 1981. The brewery operates from a beautifully restored heritage building and offers guided tours. The tasting room pours a range of beers. Nelson's hop-growing heritage is on display throughout — the region produces the majority of New Zealand's hops.

reviews.summary: Great tour through NZ craft beer history. The tasting room is relaxed and the beers are consistently good.

pois.name: Farewell Spit Nature Reserve
pois.short: 26km sand spit and internationally significant wetland bird habitat.
pois.commentary: Farewell Spit is a remarkable 26-kilometre sandspit curving into Golden Bay, forming one of the most important wading bird habitats in the Southern Hemisphere. The spit hosts over 90 bird species, including bar-tailed godwits that migrate 11,000 kilometres non-stop from Alaska. Access is restricted to licensed tour operators who run 4WD trips to the lighthouse and gannet colony.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary birding destination. The 4WD tour to the lighthouse is the only way to see the spit properly.

pois.name: Te Waikoropupu Springs / Pupu Springs
pois.short: Among the world's clearest freshwater springs, sacred to local Maori.
pois.commentary: Te Waikoropupu Springs near Takaka are among the clearest freshwater springs on Earth, with underwater visibility measured at up to 63 metres. The springs pump out around 14,000 litres per second of crystal-clear water. A boardwalk loop track (30 minutes) leads through native bush to viewing platforms. The springs are wahi tapu (sacred) to local Maori and swimming is strictly prohibited.

reviews.summary: The water clarity is mesmerising — like looking through glass. A quick but unforgettable stop. Respect the wahi tapu status.

pois.name: Hop Federation Brewery / Riwaka
pois.short: Craft brewery in NZ's hop-growing heartland with tastings and beer garden.
pois.commentary: Hop Federation is a craft brewery in Riwaka, at the heart of New Zealand's hop-growing region, where over 90 per cent of the country's hops are cultivated. The brewery's location allows it to use ultra-fresh local hops, including the internationally acclaimed Nelson Sauvin and Motueka varieties. The tasting room and beer garden offer flights of the current range, which changes seasonally. The setting among hop gardens and apple orchards gives context to the raw ingredients. Riwaka is also the gateway to the Abel Tasman National Park, making the brewery a natural pre or post-adventure stop. The region's other craft breweries, including Eddyline, Golden Bear, and Townshend, make a Nelson beer trail a viable alternative to wine touring.

reviews.summary: Excellent craft beer in the heart of hop country. The freshness of the local hops makes a real difference. Lovely beer garden setting.

pois.name: Wharariki Beach / Golden Bay
pois.short: Wild, remote beach with towering rock archways, sand dunes, and seal pups.
pois.commentary: Wharariki Beach at the remote northern tip of Golden Bay is one of New Zealand's most dramatic beaches — a sweep of golden sand punctuated by towering offshore rock archways and flanked by monumental sand dunes. The beach is reached by a 20-minute walk through farmland from the car park, which adds to the sense of discovery. At the western end, a fur seal colony produces pups in winter who play in tidal pools. The wind-sculpted sand dunes behind the beach create a constantly shifting landscape. The beach is wild and unswimmable due to strong currents, but for photographers and walkers it is one of the most spectacular coastal landscapes in the country. The remoteness — it's an hour's drive from Takaka — keeps visitor numbers manageable.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's most dramatic and photogenic beaches. The rock archways are extraordinary. Seal pups in winter are enchanting.

pois.name: Riuwaka Resurgence / Te Puna o Riuwaka
pois.short: Sacred spring where the Riuwaka River emerges from underground — a wahi tapu of Te Ātiawa.
pois.commentary: Te Puna o Riuwaka is where the Riuwaka River spectacularly resurges from an underground cave system at the base of the Tākaka Hill. The spring, which pumps crystal-clear water from marble caves, is recognised as wahi tapu (sacred place) by Te Ātiawa and Ngāti Rārua iwi. A short walking track leads to the resurgence pool, which is a stunning natural feature — the water is impossibly clear and the volume emerging from the rock face is impressive. The site connects to a vast underground cave network extending deep under the Tākaka Hill marble belt. Visitors should treat the area with respect as a culturally significant site.

reviews.summary: A remarkable natural feature with deep cultural significance. The water clarity is extraordinary. Treat with respect.

pois.name: Waka Abel Tasman — Māori Guided Tour
pois.short: Explore Abel Tasman National Park by traditional waka with Māori guides sharing ancestral stories.
pois.commentary: Waka Abel Tasman offers a unique way to experience the national park — by traditional Māori waka (canoe), guided by tangata whenua who share the stories of their ancestors and the cultural significance of the landscape. The tours paddle through the park's golden beaches and turquoise waters while guides point out traditional food-gathering sites, pa locations, and explain the Māori history that predates European contact by centuries. The experience provides cultural context that transforms a scenic paddle into a journey through living history. Tours cater to all experience levels and provide a perspective on the park that standard kayak tours cannot offer.

reviews.summary: A deeply enriching cultural experience. The guides' knowledge transforms the landscape. Suitable for all abilities.

pois.name: Nelson Classic Car Museum
pois.short: Impressive collection of vintage and classic cars in downtown Nelson.
pois.commentary: The Nelson Classic Car Museum houses an impressive collection of over 150 vintage, classic, and exotic cars spanning automotive history from the early 1900s to modern supercars. The collection includes rare and unusual vehicles alongside well-known classics. The museum is well laid out with informative displays explaining each vehicle's significance. For car enthusiasts, highlights include rare pre-war vehicles, American muscle cars, and a growing collection of motorsport memorabilia. Even non-car-people find the beautifully restored vehicles and the stories behind them engaging.

reviews.summary: Impressive collection even for non-car-enthusiasts. Well presented with good information. A good rainy-day option in Nelson.

pois.name: Split Apple Rock / Tokangawha
pois.short: Iconic boulder split perfectly in two in the waters of Abel Tasman — accessible by kayak or water taxi.
pois.commentary: Split Apple Rock (Tokangawha) is one of the Abel Tasman coast's most photographed features — a massive granite boulder split perfectly in half, sitting in the shallow waters of Tasman Bay. The rock's formation is geological (differential weathering along a fracture plane), but in Māori legend, two gods fought over the boulder and split it with their great strength. The rock is accessible by sea kayak, water taxi, or a short walk at low tide from Kaiteriteri. Kayaking past the rock is one of the Abel Tasman's defining experiences, with the golden beaches and clear water of the coast providing a stunning backdrop.

reviews.summary: Iconic and photogenic natural feature. Best seen from a kayak. The Abel Tasman coast around it is beautiful.

pois.name: Centre of New Zealand Walk — Nelson
pois.short: Short walk to the marker at the geographic centre of New Zealand with panoramic views over Nelson.
pois.commentary: The Centre of New Zealand walk is a short (30-minute) climb from the Botanical Reserve to the summit marker that indicates the geographic centre of New Zealand. While the marker's accuracy is debatable (it was actually a trigonometric survey point), the walk provides excellent panoramic views over Nelson city, Tasman Bay, and the surrounding ranges. The track passes through native bush and exotic plantings in the Botanical Reserve. At the summit, 360-degree views extend from the Richmond Ranges to the coast. It's a popular local exercise walk and an easy way to orient yourself to Nelson's geography. The views are particularly good at sunset.

reviews.summary: Quick walk with great views. The 'centre of NZ' claim is fun. Popular local exercise route. Good at sunset.

pois.name: Jester House Café & Eels
pois.short: Eccentric garden café with tame eels you can hand-feed — beloved family destination.
pois.commentary: Jester House is one of Nelson-Tasman's most beloved family destinations — an eccentric garden café near Māpua where the main attraction (besides excellent food) is the colony of tame longfin eels that can be hand-fed in the garden stream. The eels glide up to the stream edge and gently take food from outstretched hands — a unique and slightly thrilling experience, especially for children. The garden also features quirky sculptures, a playground, and resident animals. The café serves excellent food using local ingredients. The combination of good food, eccentric character, and the eel-feeding experience makes Jester House a genuine one-of-a-kind destination.

reviews.summary: Delightfully eccentric. The eel feeding is unique and thrilling. Excellent café food. Kids absolutely love it.

pois.name: The Suter Art Gallery / Te Aratoi o Whakatū
pois.short: Nelson's premier public art gallery overlooking Queens Gardens.
pois.commentary: The Suter Art Gallery, also known as Te Aratoi o Whakatū, is Nelson's premier public art gallery, beautifully situated overlooking the historic Queens Gardens. Reopened in 2016 after a major redevelopment, the gallery houses a significant permanent collection spanning early colonial landscapes to contemporary New Zealand art, with rotating exhibitions throughout the year. The gallery has a particular strength in works by Nelson region artists, reflecting the area's long tradition as a creative hub. The on-site café spills out into the gardens and is a popular lunch spot. Free entry to most exhibitions. The gallery shop stocks locally made art, jewellery, and design pieces.

reviews.summary: Art lovers appreciate the quality exhibitions and the beautiful garden setting. The café is highly rated, and free entry makes it an easy stop in central Nelson.

pois.name: Nelson Art Trail / Creative Pathways
pois.short: Self-guided trail through dozens of artist studios and galleries.
pois.commentary: The Nelson Art Trail is a self-guided tour connecting dozens of working artist studios, galleries, and creative spaces across the Nelson and Tasman regions. Nelson has one of the highest concentrations of working artists per capita in New Zealand, with craftspeople working in glass, ceramics, textiles, painting, sculpture, and jewellery. The trail map (available from the i-SITE or online) routes visitors through the city centre, up into the hills around Founders Heritage Park, and out to the artistic community of Upper Moutere. Many studios welcome drop-in visitors, offering a chance to see artists at work and purchase directly. The trail works well over a full day or can be sampled in a few hours.

reviews.summary: Visitors love meeting artists in their studios and the variety of creative disciplines on display. A unique way to experience Nelson's artistic soul.

pois.name: Upper Moutere Wine Village
pois.short: Cluster of boutique wineries and a craft brewery in NZ's oldest village.
pois.commentary: Upper Moutere (formerly Sarau) is one of the oldest European settlements in New Zealand, established by German immigrants in the 1840s, and has evolved into a charming wine and food destination. The village and surrounding area host a cluster of acclaimed boutique wineries including Neudorf Vineyards, known for world-class chardonnay and pinot noir, and Kahurangi Estate. The Moutere Inn, established in 1850, claims to be New Zealand's oldest pub and serves craft beers alongside hearty meals. The rolling hills, hop gardens, and fruit orchards create a distinctly European pastoral landscape. Cellar doors are generally open daily in summer, weekends in winter. Combine with the Nelson Art Trail for a perfect day out.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers praise the quality of the boutique wines and the relaxed cellar door experiences. The Moutere Inn is a must-visit. Beautiful rural setting.

pois.name: Lake Angelus Track / Angelus Circuit
pois.short: Three-day alpine circuit to a stunning lake in Nelson Lakes National Park.
pois.commentary: The Lake Angelus Track is one of New Zealand's finest alpine tramping experiences, climbing from the shores of Lake Rotoiti through beech forest to the spectacular tussock-fringed Lake Angelus at 1,650m elevation. The DOC-managed Angelus Hut sits on the lake's edge, offering one of the most scenic overnight stays in the country. The standard circuit takes 2-3 days and traverses the Robert Ridge, providing sweeping views across Nelson Lakes National Park. The alpine section is exposed and requires good fitness and navigation skills. Snow can linger until November and return by April. Book hut passes through DOC well in advance as it's increasingly popular. A helicopter option exists for those wanting to skip the climb.

reviews.summary: Trampers describe Lake Angelus as one of NZ's most beautiful alpine settings. The Robert Ridge traverse is challenging but spectacular. Hut bookings are essential.

pois.name: Janie Seddon Shipwreck
pois.short: Photogenic rusting shipwreck on the shore at Motueka.
pois.commentary: The Janie Seddon is a rusting steel shipwreck sitting on the shore near Motueka, one of several decommissioned vessels left along the Nelson-Tasman coast. Built in 1901 as a steam-powered cargo ship, the Janie Seddon served various roles before being deliberately beached. Now heavily rusted and partially collapsed, the wreck has become a popular photography subject, especially at sunset when the warm light catches the corroded steel. It's easily accessible from a short walkway near the Motueka waterfront. While not a major attraction, the shipwreck adds atmosphere to a coastal walk and pairs well with a visit to the Motueka Sunday Market. Viewable at all tides.

reviews.summary: Photographers enjoy the dramatic rusted textures against coastal scenery. A quick but atmospheric stop. Best at golden hour.

pois.name: Dancing Sands Distillery, Tākaka
pois.short: A husband-and-wife gin distillery in Tākaka using native botanicals and pure Golden Bay water to craft award-winning spirits.
pois.commentary: Dancing Sands is the passion project of Ben and Sarah Bonoma, who distil gin, vodka and limoncello in their Tākaka workshop using water from the nearby Tākaka River and native botanicals like kawakawa, horopito and mānuka. Their Sun Kissed Gin won gold at the International Wine & Spirits Competition. The tasting room is intimate and the couple are generous with their time, walking visitors through the distillation process and the stories behind each botanical. It's the perfect Golden Bay afternoon stop.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the personal touch and the quality of the spirits. The native botanical gins are standout. Some note limited opening hours — check ahead.

pois.name: Coppermine Trail Mountain Bike Ride
pois.short: One of New Zealand's most accessible alpine mountain bike rides, starting just 15 minutes from downtown Nelson.
pois.commentary: The Coppermine Trail climbs from the Maitai Valley behind Nelson into the mineral-rich hills where copper was once mined. The ride takes you through regenerating bush to subalpine meadows with sweeping views over Tasman Bay and the mountains beyond. It's graded intermediate and the climb is steady rather than brutal, making it one of the most accessible alpine rides in the country. The descent is fast and flowing. You can extend the ride by linking with the Dun Mountain Trail for a full-day epic.

reviews.summary: Mountain bikers praise the views and the variety of terrain. The accessibility from Nelson city is a major plus. Some sections can be muddy after rain.

pois.name: Tākaka Hill Marble & Karst Landscape
pois.short: The winding drive over Tākaka Hill passes through one of New Zealand's most dramatic karst landscapes — marble outcrops, sinkholes and cave systems.
pois.commentary: Tākaka Hill (SH60) is the gateway to Golden Bay and crosses one of the most geologically interesting landscapes in New Zealand. The road winds through ancient marble formations, past sinkholes, disappearing streams and the entrance to Ngarua Caves. The marble here is Ordovician — around 450 million years old — and has been sculpted by water into dramatic karst formations. Stop at the various lookout points for views over both bays. The Hawkes Lookout near the summit offers one of the finest panoramas in the Top of the South.

reviews.summary: The drive is scenic but winding — not for nervous drivers. The geological formations are fascinating. Hawkes Lookout is a highlight. Allow extra time for stops.

pois.name: Murchison Whitewater Kayaking & Rafting
pois.short: The four-rivers plain around Murchison offers some of the best all-grades white-water kayaking and rafting in New Zealand.
pois.commentary: Murchison sits at the confluence of four rivers — the Buller, Matakitaki, Mangles and Matiri — making it the white-water capital of the South Island. The rivers offer everything from gentle Grade 1 family floats to serious Grade 4-5 kayaking for experts. The Buller River's Earthquake Section is legendary among New Zealand paddlers, while the Matakitaki is a stunning wilderness run. Several operators offer guided rafting trips suitable for beginners. The town itself is small and unpretentious — a genuine river-community vibe.

reviews.summary: Paddlers rate Murchison as a world-class destination. The variety of rivers and grades is unmatched. Guides are experienced and safety-focused. The town has limited amenities but good camping.

pois.name: Tōtaranui Beach & Campground
pois.short: A sweeping golden beach at the northern end of Abel Tasman National Park — one of New Zealand's finest DOC campgrounds.
pois.commentary: Tōtaranui is a broad crescent of golden sand at the northern reach of Abel Tasman National Park, backed by native bush and reached by a winding gravel road from Tākaka. The DOC campground here is one of the best in the country — basic but beautifully positioned right on the beach. The swimming is excellent in the sheltered bay, and the Abel Tasman Coast Track passes through for day-walk options in both directions. Book well ahead for summer; off-season it can be blissfully empty. The road in is an adventure in itself.

reviews.summary: The beach is stunning and the campground perfectly located. Book months in advance for Christmas holidays. The access road is narrow but sealed. Facilities are basic but well-maintained.

pois.name: Harwoods Hole
pois.short: New Zealand's deepest vertical cave shaft — a 176-metre plunge into darkness reached by a bush walk through Tākaka Hill marble country.
pois.commentary: Harwoods Hole is a massive tomo (vertical cave entrance) dropping 176 metres straight down into the Starlight Cave system beneath Tākaka Hill. The walk from Canaan Downs car park takes about an hour through subalpine scrub and marble karst landscape. You can peer into the shaft from a fenced lookout — dropping a stone and counting the seconds before impact gives a visceral sense of the depth. The surrounding Canaan Downs offer excellent camping and walking, and the area feels wonderfully remote despite being only an hour from Nelson.

reviews.summary: The hole is genuinely awe-inspiring — the depth is hard to comprehend. The walk is moderate and scenic. Some find the lookout fence insufficient. Not suitable for those with vertigo.

pois.name: Motueka Salt Water Baths & Foreshore
pois.short: Heritage salt-water swimming baths on Motueka's foreshore — a slice of old New Zealand seaside culture.
pois.commentary: The Motueka Salt Water Swimming Baths are a relic of an era when every New Zealand coastal town had its own tidal pool. The concrete pool fills and drains with the tide, offering sheltered salt-water swimming with views across Tasman Bay. The surrounding foreshore walk is pleasant and the town's cafés and galleries are a short stroll away. It's free to use and particularly lovely on a calm summer evening when the bay turns gold. A genuine piece of heritage that most visitors to the Abel Tasman gateway drive straight past.

reviews.summary: A charming heritage feature. Best at high tide when the pool is full. The foreshore walk is lovely. Some find the pool basic but that's part of its appeal.

pois.name: Nelson Art & Craft Trail
pois.short: Drive between open studios and galleries across the Nelson-Tasman region — one of New Zealand's densest concentrations of working artists.
pois.commentary: Nelson-Tasman has more working artists per capita than anywhere else in New Zealand, and the Art & Craft Trail connects dozens of open studios and galleries scattered across the region. Expect glass-blowers, potters, jewellers, printmakers, sculptors and painters — many international-calibre — working in studios you can visit. Highlights include Höglund Art Glass in Richmond, the textile artists of Moutere, and the potters' studios along the Great Taste Trail route. Many studios welcome drop-in visitors, but a few work by appointment. The trail brochure is available at the Nelson i-SITE.

reviews.summary: A wonderful way to discover local artists. The quality is consistently high. Some studios are appointment-only so check ahead. The trail brochure is essential for navigation. Allow a full day.

pois.name: Canaan Downs Campsite & Walks
pois.short: A free DOC campsite in an elevated karst meadow on Tākaka Hill — stunning geology and the trailhead for Harwoods Hole.
pois.commentary: Canaan Downs sits in a wide, grassy basin high on Tākaka Hill, surrounded by marble outcrops and karst formations. The free DOC campsite is basic but beautifully positioned, and several walking tracks radiate from it — including the route to Harwoods Hole and various loops through the marble landscape. The downs themselves are an unusual ecosystem: tussock meadow on marble bedrock, dotted with sinkholes and disappearing streams. On clear nights the stargazing is exceptional, with minimal light pollution. It's one of those places that's worth visiting for the landscape alone, even without the walks.

reviews.summary: A beautiful and unique camping spot. The geological landscape is fascinating. Free and rarely crowded. Basic facilities only. The access road is narrow but manageable.

pois.name: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary Walk
pois.short: A predator-fenced sanctuary on Nelson's doorstep where native birds thrive — South Island robin, bellbird, kākā and more.
pois.commentary: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary is one of New Zealand's largest predator-fenced mainland sanctuaries, protecting 700 hectares of native forest just minutes from central Nelson. Inside the fence, native bird populations are recovering rapidly — South Island robin, bellbird, tūī, kererū and the recently reintroduced South Island kākā are all present. Walking tracks range from 30-minute loops to half-day adventures through mature beech and podocarp forest. The birds here are notably bold and curious, especially the robins, which will approach to within arm's reach. Night tours offer encounters with little spotted kiwi and morepork.

reviews.summary: The birdsong inside the fence is remarkable. Robins are extremely friendly and photogenic. Well-maintained tracks. A great introduction to native conservation. Night tours for kiwi are excellent.

pois.name: Farewell Spit Bird Sanctuary Tour
pois.short: A guided tour of one of the world's great bird sanctuaries — a 34 km sand spit hosting 90+ migratory and wetland bird species.
pois.commentary: Farewell Spit (Onetahua) is a 34 km arc of sand extending from the northwestern tip of the South Island, forming one of the most important wetland bird habitats in the Southern Hemisphere. Access beyond the public walking area is by guided tour only, with operators running 4WD trips along the spit to the lighthouse and bird hides. Over 90 species have been recorded, with bar-tailed godwits, knots, oystercatchers and numerous tern and gull species present in vast numbers. The spit is particularly spectacular from September to March when Arctic migrants swell the populations. The gannet colony at the tip is the only mainland colony in the South Island.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary birding experience. The guided tours are essential for access. The scale of the bird populations is impressive. Best during migration season. The landscape itself is hauntingly beautiful.

pois.name: Māpua Wharf Dining Precinct
pois.short: A cluster of excellent eateries on a restored estuary wharf — smoked fish, craft beer, ice cream and waterfront dining at its best.
pois.commentary: Māpua Wharf has been transformed from a derelict commercial wharf into one of the Nelson-Tasman region's best dining destinations. The standout is The Smokehouse, where fish is smoked on-site over mānuka sawdust and served with views over the Waimea Estuary. Alongside it you'll find craft beer at Golden Bear Brewing, excellent gelato, a produce store, and several cafés and restaurants. The wharf sits at the start of the Great Taste Trail, making it the perfect refuelling stop for cyclists. At high tide, the estuary laps at the wharf's edge; at low tide, the mudflats host wading birds. It's a quintessential Nelson-Tasman afternoon.

reviews.summary: The Smokehouse is a highlight. The waterfront setting is lovely. Great variety of food options. Popular on weekends. A perfect lunch stop on the Great Taste Trail.

pois.name: Kiwi Spirit Distillery
pois.short: Home of the only agave farm outside Mexico, producing unique New Zealand spirits.
pois.commentary: Kiwi Spirit Distillery in Golden Bay is home to the only commercial tequila-style spirit farm outside of Mexico. The Knight family grows agave plants in Golden Bay's mild climate, producing distinctive spirits alongside gin and vodka in their copper pot still.

reviews.summary: Unique agave spirits — truly one of a kind in NZ.

pois.name: The Mussel Inn
pois.short: Legendary Golden Bay bush pub brewing its own beer with regular live music.
pois.commentary: Tucked into the bush at Onekaka, The Mussel Inn is an iconic Golden Bay institution. This off-grid pub brews its own range of beers on-site, serves hearty food with local mussels, and hosts regular live music sessions attracting colourful characters from across the region.

reviews.summary: Quintessential Golden Bay — great beer, great vibe.

pois.name: Rameka Track — Tākaka Hill
pois.short: Backcountry track over Tākaka Hill linking Nelson Lakes to Golden Bay.
pois.commentary: The Rameka Track is a historic pack track crossing the marble karst landscape of Tākaka Hill. Used by gold miners in the 1800s, it now offers trampers and mountain bikers a challenging route through beech forest, limestone formations, and subalpine clearings.

reviews.summary: Rugged backcountry track with fascinating geology.

pois.name: The Grove Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Otherworldly limestone formations hidden in coastal bush near Tākaka.
pois.commentary: A short walk through nikau palm forest leads to a labyrinth of towering limestone outcrops, some over 15 metres tall. These dramatic karst formations were carved by acidic rainwater over millions of years, creating a natural sculpture garden unlike anything else in the region.
