reviews.summary: Amazing limestone formations — feels like another world.

pois.name: Mapua Wharf & Creative Precinct
pois.short: Charming waterside precinct with seafood restaurants, galleries, and an estuary walk.
pois.commentary: Mapua Wharf has been transformed from a derelict industrial site into a vibrant waterside destination. Local seafood restaurants, craft galleries, and artisan shops line the wharf, while the estuary walk leads to a nature reserve teeming with wading birds.

reviews.summary: Lovely wharf precinct — the seafood and estuary walk are highlights.

pois.name: Wainui Falls Walk — Abel Tasman
pois.short: Bush walk through nikau palms to a waterfall via a swing bridge in Golden Bay.
pois.commentary: The Wainui Falls track crosses a swing bridge and passes through lush nikau palm forest to reach a 20-metre waterfall cascading into a rock pool. An easy family walk near Tākaka, it showcases Golden Bay's remarkable coastal bush in a short, rewarding loop.

reviews.summary: Beautiful bush walk — the nikau palms and waterfall are lovely.

pois.name: Cobb Valley / Kahurangi National Park
pois.short: A high, less-crowded Kahurangi gateway with a reservoir road, alpine plants, huts and long-valley tramping.
pois.commentary: Cobb Valley is one of the great inland side roads of Nelson Tasman. From Upper Tākaka the road climbs toward the Cobb Reservoir, then becomes a gateway into Kahurangi National Park: Trilobite Hut, Chaffey Hut, Lake Cobb, Fenella Hut and a web of longer tramping routes beyond. Even for day visitors, the valley gives a rare mix of hydro history, marble country, beech forest, tussock and alpine botany without the traffic of better-known coastal parks. DOC warns that the road is narrow, unsealed in places and demanding in winter, so the journey is part of the experience.

reviews.summary: Loved by trampers and photographers for big landscapes, botany and hut access; the access road is the main planning variable.

pois.name: Pupu Hydro Walkway
pois.short: A Golden Bay loop following a historic water race to an early hydro scheme above Tākaka.
pois.commentary: The Pupu Hydro Walkway is part engineering lesson, part bush walk. The track follows old water-race infrastructure that once turned falling water into electricity, and the steady gradient gives time to appreciate how much patience early small-scale power demanded.

pois.name: Māpua Ferry Crossing
pois.short: A short bike-and-passenger ferry link that makes Tasman’s Great Taste Trail feel like a proper journey.
pois.commentary: The Māpua ferry is small, practical and oddly delightful. Bikes roll on, the estuary opens around you, and in a few minutes the Great Taste Trail turns from path to crossing, giving the ride a tiny maritime adventure.

pois.name: Nelson Provincial Museum
pois.short: A central Nelson museum connecting regional art, settlement, photography and community memory.
pois.commentary: Nelson Provincial Museum is the town’s memory bank. Its exhibitions move between taonga, photography, settler history and contemporary regional stories, giving context to a place visitors often experience only as a sunny gateway to national parks.

pois.name: Riuwaka Resurgence
pois.short: A short walk to cold, clear water emerging from Tākaka Hill’s marble cave systems.
pois.commentary: Riuwaka Resurgence is a place to arrive quietly. Water emerges intensely clear from the marble mountain, cold from its underground journey; it is beautiful, culturally significant and best visited with restraint rather than noise.

pois.name: Abel Tasman Canyons
pois.short: Guided canyoning through granite streams, pools and waterfalls near Abel Tasman National Park.
pois.commentary: Abel Tasman is usually sold as beaches and kayaks, but the canyoning adds a steeper, colder thrill. Guided trips turn forest streams, jumps, slides and abseils into a full-body way to meet the national park’s inland edges.

pois.short: Tāhunanui Beach is Nelson’s broad, social beach playground, where shallow water, cafés and sunset light make it much more than a place to swim.

pois.short: McKee Memorial Reserve is a gentle Tasman Bay coastal stop, loved for camping, swimming and the kind of sunset that makes dinner wait.

pois.short: Miyazu Japanese Garden is a small Nelson gesture of friendship, using water, stone and planting to mark the city’s sister-city relationship with Miyazu.

pois.name: Labyrinth Rocks Park
pois.short: A free limestone maze near Takaka, with narrow rock corridors and playful short walks.
pois.commentary: Labyrinth Rocks Park turns Golden Bay limestone into a natural puzzle. It is an easy stop, but the narrow corridors, mossy walls and little surprises make it feel delightfully exploratory, especially for families who want wonder without a full-day track.
pois.audio_story: Labyrinth Rocks Park turns Golden Bay limestone into a natural puzzle. It is an easy stop, but the narrow corridors, mossy walls and little surprises make it feel delightfully exploratory, especially for families who want wonder without a full-day track.

pois.name: Rawhiti Cave
pois.short: A steep Golden Bay walk to a limestone cave mouth with remarkable phytokarst formations.
pois.commentary: Rawhiti Cave asks for a proper climb, then rewards it with one of Golden Bay’s strangest cave entrances. The formations seem to grow toward the light, making the cave feel alive at the edge between underground darkness and forest air.
pois.audio_story: Rawhiti Cave asks for a proper climb, then rewards it with one of Golden Bay’s strangest cave entrances. The formations seem to grow toward the light, making the cave feel alive at the edge between underground darkness and forest air.

pois.name: Flamedaisy Glass Design
pois.short: A gallery stop that broadens the arts trail through Nelson.
